rubric.question_to_annotator: Does the model's output still assert the must_persist fact (a fact that should survive the correction unchanged)?
rubric.answer_YES: the fact is present and stated as live (restated in different words but same meaning = YES/kept).
rubric.answer_NO: the fact is absent or contradicted.

instructions: You are validating an AI model's revision behavior. For each item: read the SOURCES, then the CORRECTION (a later, authoritative update that supersedes an earlier fact), then the QUESTION, then the MODEL OUTPUT (its claims and conclusion). Apply the rubric to the SCORED SENTENCE only. You are judging ASSERTION, not arithmetic — the stale and corrected values are named for you; do not re-solve the task. Put YES/NO/UNSURE in label.judgment, your id in label.annotator_id, and any note in label.notes. Do not consult other annotators. You must pass the 6-item calibration set first.

sources.text: Refurbishment contractor Pallister Co. has erected a single tower scaffold along the east elevation of the Grendon block, loaded with render bags and a 4-person plastering crew for the day's work.

This scaffold's design rates each working bay for a maximum load of 300 kg, per the erection certificate. The render bags loaded onto the working bay plus the 4-person crew and their tools add up to a measured 380 kg. The erection certificate states loading above the rated bay maximum invalidates the scaffold's handover certificate.

At this scaffold's height, the erection standard requires tie spacing no greater than 4 metres vertically to the building face. The as-built survey found the top two lifts tied at a 6-metre spacing due to a missed tie point. The erection standard states a scaffold outside the required tie spacing may not be used until re-tied.

Working-at-height regulations require a minimum guardrail height of 950 mm on any working platform above 2 metres. The top platform's guardrail, as erected, measures only 700 mm due to a substituted shorter standard. Regulations state a platform below the minimum guardrail height may not be occupied.

correction.text: Design update: a revised bay configuration with additional standards was certified, rating each working bay for a maximum load of 400 kg, replacing the 300 kg figure.

presented.question: Considering the scaffold's rated working load per bay, the scaffold's maximum permitted tie spacing for this height, and the minimum guardrail height required at this working platform, can the crew work from the scaffold as erected, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: Document 4 certifies a revised bay configuration rating each working bay for 400 kg, replacing the earlier 300 kg rating from document 1.
The original 300 kg per-bay rating and the resulting conclusion that the 380 kg load invalidates the handover certificate are now superseded and treated as false.
The render bags, 4-person crew, and tools together measure 380 kg on the working bay.
Under the corrected 400 kg bay rating, the 380 kg load does not exceed the maximum permitted load, so the loading does not invalidate the handover certificate.
The erection standard requires tie spacing no greater than 4 metres vertically at this scaffold's height.
The as-built survey found the top two lifts tied at 6-metre spacing due to a missed tie point, exceeding the 4-metre maximum.
The erection standard states a scaffold outside the required tie spacing may not be used until re-tied.
Because the tie spacing exceeds the permitted maximum, the scaffold may not be used until it is re-tied.
Working-at-height regulations require a minimum guardrail height of 950 mm on any working platform above 2 metres.
The top platform's guardrail as erected measures only 700 mm due to a substituted shorter standard, below the required minimum.
Regulations state a platform below the minimum guardrail height may not be occupied.
Because the guardrail height is below the required minimum, the top platform may not be occupied.
Even though the corrected 400 kg bay load rating removes the loading violation, the scaffold remains non-compliant due to the tie spacing violation and the guardrail height violation.

model_output.conclusion: No, the crew cannot work from the scaffold as erected: although the corrected 400 kg bay rating means the 380 kg load is no longer a violation, the scaffold's top two lifts are tied at 6 metres (exceeding the 4-metre maximum) and the top platform's guardrail is only 700 mm (below the required 950 mm), so the scaffold must be re-tied and the guardrail corrected before the platform can be used or occupied.

presented.must_persist_fact: The crew working from the scaffold is 4 plasterers.

sources.text: Haulier Denwick is contracted to move a single 38-tonne electrical transformer from the Padgate yard to the Ellery substation. The client-fixed route runs through one weighbridge at the yard exit, across one river bridge at the midpoint, and through a short urban high-street section near the substation.

The assigned tractor-trailer combination carries a plating certificate stating a certified maximum gross combination weight of 44 tonnes for road use. The transformer plus the empty tractor and trailer weigh a measured 47 tonnes on the yard weighbridge. Operating above the certified maximum gross weight is an offence and voids the trip insurance.

The river bridge on the route carries a structural weight posting. The highway authority's current records confirm a 40-tonne maximum for any single vehicle or combination crossing the span. The authority states that no alternative river crossing exists within the permitted haulage corridor for a load of this class.

The urban high-street section is governed by a police-administered abnormal-load rule. As currently published, any load exceeding 3.5 metres in width must be accompanied by a police escort through the high street. The transformer on its trailer cradle presents a measured overall width of 4.1 metres.

correction.text: Certification update: the tractor-trailer combination was re-plated at a fresh inspection, certifying a maximum gross combination weight of 48 tonnes for road use.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 3 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the vehicle's certified gross weight, the river bridge's posted limit, and the urban high-street escort rule, can the transformer move proceed as planned on this route, and if not, what stops it?

claims.text: The transformer move covers a route with a weighbridge, a river bridge, and an urban high-street section.
The measured combined weight of the transformer, tractor, and trailer is 47 tonnes.
The original plating certificate of 44 tonnes maximum gross combination weight is superseded by a re-plating at a fresh inspection.
The current certified maximum gross combination weight for the tractor-trailer combination is 48 tonnes.
Since the measured weight of 47 tonnes is below the current certified maximum of 48 tonnes, the vehicle is not in breach of its weight certification.
The river bridge's original weight posting of 40 tonnes is superseded by a strengthening retrofit that raised the posting.
The current weight posting for the river bridge is 50 tonnes maximum for a single vehicle or combination.
The measured 47-tonne combination weight is below the current 50-tonne river bridge posting, so the bridge crossing is not restricted by weight.
The transformer's overall width on its trailer cradle is measured at 4.1 metres.
The original escort rule requiring police escort for loads over 3.5 metres wide is superseded by an amendment to the rule.
The current urban high-street rule requires a police escort only for loads exceeding 4.5 metres in width.
Since the transformer's width of 4.1 metres does not exceed the current 4.5-metre threshold, no police escort is required for the high-street section.

model_output.conclusion: Given the updated 48-tonne vehicle certification, the raised 50-tonne river bridge limit, and the amended 4.5-metre escort threshold, the transformer's 47-tonne weight and 4.1-metre width fall within all current limits, so the move can proceed as planned on this route without any restriction.

presented.must_persist_fact: No alternative river crossing exists within the permitted haulage corridor.

sources.text: Ambulance dispatch for the Fenmoor rural district is assigning a single crew to a priority call at Struan Farm, a fixed 22-kilometre road distance from the nearest station.

The trust's response-time target for this call priority is a maximum of 18 minutes from dispatch. At the posted rural road speed limits, the 22-kilometre distance to Struan Farm is calculated to take 24 minutes. The trust's policy states a call may not be assigned to a crew whose calculated response time exceeds the target.

Working-time rules require a mandatory rest break once a crew reaches 10 hours of continuous duty. The assigned crew has already logged 10.5 hours of continuous duty this shift. The rules state a crew beyond the duty-hours threshold may not be assigned a new call before their rest break.

The assigned ambulance has a remaining fuel range of 40 kilometres. The round trip to Struan Farm plus the onward transfer to Fenmoor District Hospital totals a calculated 58 kilometres. Trust vehicle policy states a vehicle may not be dispatched on a trip exceeding its remaining fuel range without refuelling first.

correction.text: Policy update: the trust reclassified this call to a rural-distance exception band with a response-time target of 26 minutes, replacing the 18-minute figure.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 3 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the trust's maximum permitted response-time target for this call priority, the assigned crew's remaining duty-hours before a mandatory rest break, and the ambulance's fuel range against the round trip plus onward hospital transfer, can this crew take the call as assigned, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: The distance from the nearest station to Struan Farm is 22 kilometres, calculated to take 24 minutes at posted rural road speed limits.
The trust reclassified this call to a rural-distance exception band with a response-time target of 26 minutes, replacing the original 18-minute target.
Since the calculated 24-minute response time does not exceed the corrected 26-minute target, the response-time policy does not block this assignment.
The assigned crew has logged 10.5 hours of continuous duty this shift.
A duty-hours extension was authorised, raising the mandatory rest break threshold to 11 hours, replacing the original 10-hour threshold.
Since the crew's 10.5 hours of continuous duty is below the corrected 11-hour threshold, the crew is not yet required to take a rest break and may be assigned a new call.
The round trip to Struan Farm plus the onward transfer to Fenmoor District Hospital totals 58 kilometres.
The ambulance topped up at the station forecourt before dispatch, raising its remaining fuel range to 60 kilometres, replacing the original 40-kilometre figure.
Since the corrected 60-kilometre fuel range exceeds the 58-kilometre round trip plus transfer distance, the fuel range policy does not block this assignment.
None of the three constraints (response-time target, duty-hours threshold, fuel range) are violated once the corrected figures are applied.

model_output.conclusion: Yes, the crew can take the call as assigned: the 24-minute response time meets the corrected 26-minute target, the crew's 10.5 duty-hours are within the corrected 11-hour threshold, and the ambulance's corrected 60-kilometre fuel range comfortably covers the 58-kilometre round trip and hospital transfer, so no policy stops the assignment.

sources.text: Flight crew Ardenmoor is sling-loading a prefabricated pylon section to a remote transmission-line tower site, a fixed single-lift job with no ground-vehicle access to the tower base.

At today's density altitude, the helicopter's external-load chart caps the maximum sling load at 1,100 kilograms. The pylon section to be lifted weighs a measured 1,350 kilograms. The flight manual states lifting above the density-altitude-adjusted maximum is outside the aircraft's certified operating envelope.

The sling set assigned to this lift carries a certified working-load limit of 1,200 kilograms. The pylon section weighs a measured 1,350 kilograms. The rigging certificate states loading a sling above its working-load limit is prohibited and voids the sling's inspection tag.

Site flight rules require a minimum of 5 kilometres visibility at the remote landing zone before a sling-load approach may begin. The morning's weather observation at the landing zone reports 3 kilometres visibility in mist. The flight rules state approaches below the minimum visibility are not authorised.

correction.text: Performance update: with a lighter fuel load carried for this sortie, the density-altitude-adjusted maximum sling load is now 1,450 kilograms, replacing the 1,100-kilogram figure.

presented.question: Considering the helicopter's rated external-load capacity at this density altitude, the sling's rated working-load limit, and the landing zone's minimum visibility rule, can the sling-load lift proceed as planned, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: Document [4] corrects the earlier density-altitude-adjusted maximum sling load figure of 1,100 kilograms stated in document [1].
With a lighter fuel load, the density-altitude-adjusted maximum sling load is now 1,450 kilograms.
The pylon section weighs a measured 1,350 kilograms.
Since 1,350 kilograms is below the corrected maximum sling load of 1,450 kilograms, the lift is within the helicopter's certified external-load envelope for density altitude.
The sling set assigned to this lift has a certified working-load limit of 1,200 kilograms.
The rigging certificate prohibits loading a sling above its working-load limit and states doing so voids the sling's inspection tag.
Since the pylon weighs 1,350 kilograms, which exceeds the sling's 1,200-kilogram working-load limit, the sling cannot legally be used for this lift.
Site flight rules require a minimum of 5 kilometres visibility at the landing zone before a sling-load approach may begin.
The morning's weather observation reports only 3 kilometres visibility in mist at the landing zone.
Since 3 kilometres is below the required 5-kilometre minimum, the approach is not authorised under current visibility conditions.
The lift is blocked by two independent factors: the sling's working-load limit being exceeded by the pylon's weight, and the landing zone visibility being below the required minimum.

model_output.conclusion: The lift cannot proceed as planned: although the helicopter's revised density-altitude external-load limit of 1,450 kilograms is now sufficient to carry the 1,350-kilogram pylon, the load still exceeds the sling's 1,200-kilogram working-load limit, and the landing zone's visibility (3 km) remains below the required 5 km minimum, so both the rigging and the weather rules prohibit the approach until a heavier-rated sling is used and visibility improves.

presented.must_persist_fact: The lift delivers a prefabricated pylon section to the remote tower site.

sources.text: Marrowfield Coal Co. mines a 70-shift extraction contract and separately runs a 3-conveyor coal-washing plant.
Extraction report: each shift extracts 210 tonnes of raw coal.
Contract note: requires 16,000 tonnes utility's minimum-tonnage supply contract; below that fails the contract.
Washing report: each of the 3 conveyors washes 60 tonnes per hour.
Capacity note: washery's hourly processing capacity is capped at 170 tonnes.
Safety-budget note: the washery's monthly safety-inspection budget is $9,000.
Budget note: requires at least $10,500 minimum monthly safety-inspection budget required.
Reference: raw coal extracted = per-shift tonnage x shifts; washing throughput = per-conveyor rate x conveyors.

correction.text: Revised Extraction report (supersedes the rate report): 250 tonnes of raw coal, replacing the 210 figure.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 2 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the raw coal extracted, the washery's hourly washing throughput, and the washery's monthly safety-inspection budget, does the scenario meet all three requirements, and if not which one fails?

claims.text: Document 8 supersedes document 1's per-shift extraction rate of 210 tonnes, replacing it with 250 tonnes.
Document 9 supersedes document 3's per-conveyor washing rate of 60 tonnes per hour, replacing it with 45 tonnes per hour.
The extraction contract covers 70 shifts.
Raw coal extracted equals per-shift tonnage multiplied by number of shifts.
Using the corrected per-shift rate of 250 tonnes, total raw coal extracted equals 250 x 70 = 17,500 tonnes.
The contract requires a minimum of 16,000 tonnes to meet the utility's minimum-tonnage supply contract.
17,500 tonnes exceeds the 16,000 tonne minimum, so the extraction requirement is met.
The washery has 3 conveyors.
Washing throughput equals per-conveyor rate multiplied by number of conveyors.
Using the corrected per-conveyor rate of 45 tonnes per hour, total washing throughput equals 45 x 3 = 135 tonnes per hour.
The washery's hourly processing capacity is capped at 170 tonnes.
135 tonnes per hour is below the 170 tonne cap, so the washing throughput requirement is met.
The washery's monthly safety-inspection budget is $9,000.
The requirement is a minimum monthly safety-inspection budget of $10,500.
$9,000 is below the required $10,500 minimum, so the safety-inspection budget requirement fails.

model_output.conclusion: After correcting the extraction rate to 250 tonnes per shift and the washing rate to 45 tonnes per hour per conveyor, the scenario meets the extraction requirement (17,500 tonnes ≥ 16,000) and the washing throughput requirement (135 tonnes/hour ≤ 170 tonnes/hour cap), but it fails the safety-inspection budget requirement because the actual $9,000 monthly budget is below the required $10,500 minimum.

presented.must_persist_fact: The washery's hourly processing capacity is 170 tonnes.

sources.text: Cragmoor Fisheries lands 3,000 crates of herring for a cannery supply contract and separately runs a 7-freezer cold-storage bay.
Landing report: each crate weighs 28.0 kilograms of herring.
Contract note: requires 76,000 kilograms cannery's minimum supply contract; below that fails the contract.
Storage report: each of the 7 freezers holds 3200 kilograms of catch.
Capacity note: cold-storage bay's total holding capacity requires at least 21,000 kilograms.
Ice-budget note: the cold-storage bay's weekly ice budget is $2,600.
Budget note: requires at least $2,900 minimum weekly ice budget required to keep the bay operating.
Reference: total herring landed = per-crate weight x crates; freezer holding load = per-freezer capacity x freezers.

correction.text: Revised Landing report (supersedes the rate report): 24.0 kilograms of herring, replacing the 28 figure.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 2 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the total herring landed, the total freezer holding load, and the cold-storage bay's weekly ice budget, does the scenario meet all three requirements, and if not which one fails?

claims.text: Document 8 supersedes document 1, correcting the per-crate herring weight from 28.0 kilograms to 24.0 kilograms.
Document 9 supersedes document 3, correcting the per-freezer capacity from 3200 kilograms to 2800 kilograms.
Total herring landed equals per-crate weight multiplied by number of crates.
Cragmoor Fisheries landed 3,000 crates of herring.
Using the corrected per-crate weight of 24.0 kilograms, total herring landed equals 24.0 x 3,000 = 72,000 kilograms.
The cannery's minimum supply contract requires at least 76,000 kilograms.
72,000 kilograms is less than the required 76,000 kilograms, so the supply contract requirement fails.
Freezer holding load equals per-freezer capacity multiplied by number of freezers.
There are 7 freezers in the cold-storage bay.
Using the corrected per-freezer capacity of 2800 kilograms, total freezer holding load equals 2800 x 7 = 19,600 kilograms.
The cold-storage bay's total holding capacity must be at least 21,000 kilograms.
19,600 kilograms is less than the required 21,000 kilograms, so the storage capacity requirement fails.
The cold-storage bay's weekly ice budget is $2,600.
The minimum weekly ice budget required to keep the bay operating is $2,900.
$2,600 is less than the required $2,900, so the ice budget requirement fails.

model_output.conclusion: After applying the corrected per-crate weight (24.0 kg) and per-freezer capacity (2800 kg), none of the three requirements are met: total herring landed is only 72,000 kg (below the 76,000 kg contract minimum), freezer holding load is only 19,600 kg (below the 21,000 kg capacity requirement), and the weekly ice budget is only $2,600 (below the $2,900 minimum), so all three requirements fail.

presented.must_persist_fact: Cragmoor Fisheries lands 3,000 crates of herring for a cannery supply contract and separately runs a 7-freezer cold-storage bay.

A survey this morning measured the gap at level 22 at only 90 mm due to platform settlement. The interlock will not release below the minimum clearance gap.

correction.text: Data-plate update: following a counterweight adjustment, the hoist is now rated for a combined maximum load of 1,150 kg for personnel-and-materials trips, replacing the 1,000 kg figure.

claims.text: The hoist is being loaded with 650 kg of drywall sheets and a 3-person crew with tools adding 450 kg, for a combined load of 1,100 kg.
The hoist's combined maximum load rating was updated to 1,150 kg for personnel-and-materials trips, replacing the earlier 1,000 kg rating.
The planned load of 1,100 kg is within the updated 1,150 kg combined maximum load rating, so the load limit is not exceeded.
The mixed-load speed cap was updated to 1.3 metres per second via certified firmware, replacing the earlier 0.75 m/s cap.
The hoist operator's control panel is currently set to 1.2 metres per second, which is within the updated 1.3 m/s mixed-load speed cap.
The level 22 landing gate interlock's minimum required clearance was reassessed at 90 mm following re-levelling and shimming, replacing the earlier 150 mm requirement.
A survey measured the actual clearance gap at level 22 to be 90 mm.
The measured 90 mm clearance gap meets the updated 90 mm minimum clearance requirement, so the interlock will release.

model_output.conclusion: Yes, the hoist run can proceed as planned: the 1,100 kg load is within the updated 1,150 kg combined load limit, the operator's 1.2 m/s speed is within the updated 1.3 m/s mixed-load speed cap, and the measured 90 mm clearance at level 22 now meets the updated 90 mm minimum interlock clearance requirement.
